original_abstract: Background: The two-stage time series design represents a powerful analytical tool in environmental epidemiology. Recently, models for both stages have been extended with the development of distributed lag non-linear models (DLNMs), a methodology for investigating simultaneously non-linear and lagged relationships, and multivariate meta-analysis, a methodology to pool estimates of multi-parameter associations. However, the application of both
humanized_abstract: Aims: The two-stage time series design has been increasingly used in environmental epidemiology. Recent advancements have provided models for each stage of this design such as distributed lag non-linear models (DLNMs) to investigate non-linear and lagged effects and multivariate meta-analysis to combine multi-parameter associations derived from single studies. However, the application of both approaches has not yet been validated. Therefore, in the present study, we aimed to investigate the reliability and applicability of these approaches in the context of two-stage time series designs.

document.output: Aims: The two-stage time series design has been increasingly used in environmental epidemiology. Recent advancements have provided models for each stage of this design such as distributed lag non-linear models (DLNMs) to investigate non-linear and lagged effects and multivariate meta-analysis to combine multi-parameter associations derived from single studies. However, the application of both approaches has not yet been validated. Therefore, in the present study, we aimed to investigate the reliability and applicability of these approaches in the context of two-stage time series designs.
